summary: Jeff Atwood highlights an annoying inconsistency between C# and VB.NET in Visual Studio 2003's Immediate Window: when printing strings containing newlines, VB.NET displays them as actual line breaks while C# shows the raw escape notation (\r\n). Despite his general agnosticism about the C# vs VB.NET debate, he finds this C# behavior counterintuitive and difficult to work with, especially since even variable tooltips exhibit the same issue. He explores potential workarounds but notes their limitations.
key_points: C# displays newlines as literal \r\n notation in the Immediate Window, while VB.NET renders them as actual line breaks
This inconsistency extends to variable tooltips in the C# IDE
The System.Diagnostics.Debug.WriteLine workaround exists but isn't always practical due to scope limitations
Sometimes VB.NET handles things more sensibly than C#, contrary to common assumptions
money_quotes: I've become rather agnostic on the whole topic of C# versus VB.NET, but there are still those annoying little differences that sneak up behind you and rabbit-punch you in the kidneys.
Usually VB.NET is guilty of handling things in odd and unexpected ways, but this time it's C#.
I expect newlines to appear in the immediate window as, oh, I don't know... NEW LINES?
It's a bit difficult to read text filled with a bunch of \r\n notation instead of human readable whitespace.
key_insight: Developer tools should prioritize human readability over technical accuracy when displaying debug output, since the whole point of debugging is to help developers understand what's happening.
